perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information” means any non-public information disclosed by Disclosing Party to Recipient, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally , visually or by inspection of tangible objects in connection with the Meeting and related discussions or the Permitted Use (including, without limitation, research, product plans, products, services, equipment, customers, markets, software, inventi ons, discoveries, ideas, processes, designs, drawings, hardware, formulations, specifications, product configuration information, product components, marketing and finance documents, prototypes, samples, data sets, and equipment), whether or not designated as “confidential” at the time of disc losure.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information” means any information disclosed by Disclosing Party to Recipient, either directly or indirectly in writing, orally, visually or by inspection of tangible objects in connection with the Meeting and related discussions or the Permitted Use (including, without limitation, research, product plans, products, services, equipment, customers, markets, software, inventi ons, discoveries, ideas, processes, designs, drawings, hardware, formulations, specifications, product configuration information, product components, marketing and finance documents, prototypes, samples, data sets, and equipment), whether or not designated as “confidential” at the time of disc losure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'non-public', the definition of Confidential Information becomes overly broad. This could include publicly available information, which is generally not protected under trade secret law. This creates a contradiction in the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines a trade secret as information that is 'sufficiently secret to derive economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use.'  The modified definition includes *any* information, not just secret information, contradicting the core principle of trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the word 'non-public' from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' The Law Explanation states that CUTSA defines a trade secret as information 'from not being generally known to the public.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines misappropriation of a 'trade secret.' By removing 'non-public,' the contract now includes publicly available information in the definition of confidential information, directly contradicting the legal requirement that a trade secret must not be generally known.

perturbation.original_text: Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information in strict confidence, in a manner consistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, and in any event no less than a reasonable standard of care and in strict accordance with the provisions of this Agreement ; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for no purpose other than the Permitted Use.
perturbation.changed_text: Representatives ”) who have a need to know the Confidential Information for use in evaluating or pursuing a potential business relationship with Disclosing Party or its affiliates (“ Permitted Use. ”) Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to (i) hold the Confidential Information with minimal oversight, in a manner inconsistent with the protections it employs to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature; and (ii) use the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for any purpose that benefits the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly contradicts the standard of care requirement for protecting confidential information. It suggests a lower level of protection and allows the recipient to use the information for any purpose beneficial to them, violating the defined 'Permitted Use'.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1708 states that 'Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his rights.'  By allowing for 'minimal oversight' and use of confidential information for the Recipient's benefit, the contract now encourages actions that could injure the Disclosing Party's property rights, contradicting this general duty of care.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1708. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1708. Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 664, Sec. 38.5. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code § 1708. The original text requires the recipient to hold confidential information in strict confidence, using a reasonable standard of care and adhering to the agreement's provisions. The changed text, however, introduces 'minimal oversight' and allows the Recipient to use the information for their own benefit, thus potentially 'injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights' as stated in the provided snippet from CIV 1708. This aligns with the perturbation explanation, which identifies a weakening of the standard of care and violation of 'Permitted Use'.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Recipient specified in Section 2 above shall not apply with respect to Confidential Information to the extent that such Confidential Information: (a) is or becomes generally known to the public without any act or omission on the part of the Recipient or its Representatives; (b) is in th e Recipient’s lawful possession at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) is or becomes known to the Recipient t hrough disclosure by an unaffiliated third party (except where such third party is known by the Recipient to be disclosing such inf ormation in breach of obligations of confidence); or (d) is independently developed by or for the Recipient by persons who have had no access to or been informed of the existence or substance of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Recipient specified in Section 2 above shall not apply with respect to Confidential Information to the extent that such Confidential Information: (a) is or becomes generally known within the Recipient's company without any act or omission on the part of the Recipient or its Representatives; (b) is in th e Recipient’s possession at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) is or becomes known to the Recipient through disclosure by any third party; or (d) is developed by or for the Recipient by persons who may have had access to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: These changes weaken the limitations on confidentiality.  Changing 'generally known to the public' to 'generally known within the Recipient's company' significantly narrows the exception. Removing 'lawful' before possession and 'unaffiliated' before third party broadens the circumstances where the confidentiality obligation doesn't apply.  Removing the clause about persons who have had no access to information also defeats the purpose of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, information must be 'not generally known to the public' to qualify as a trade secret. Narrowing the exception to 'generally known within the Recipient's company' allows the Recipient to disclose information to the public and then claim it was already known within their company, negating trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), specifically regarding the definition of a trade secret. The original text protects information 'generally known to the public,' while the changed text narrows this to 'generally known within the Recipient's company.' This directly undermines trade secret protection because information known within a company is not necessarily publicly known. The Law Explanation accurately reflects this contradiction: 'Under CUTSA, information must be 'not generally known to the public' to qualify as a trade secret.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines misappropriation and the duty to maintain secrecy, indirectly supporting that limiting the scope of what is 'generally known' weakens trade secret protection as it removes the confidentiality of information known outside the company, but not publicly.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant the Recipient any license, right, title, or interest in or to the Confidential Inform ation. The Recipient acquires no intellectual property license or rights under this Agreement except the limited right to review such Confidential Information. Neither this Agreement nor the disclosure of any Confidential Information hereunder shall result in any obligation on the part of either party to enter into any further agreement with the other or to require Disclosing Party to disclose any particular or further Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information shall remain the property of both the Disclosing Party and the Recipient and this Agreement shall be deemed to grant the Recipient equal license, right, title, or interest in or to the Confidential Information. The Recipient acquires full intellectual property license or rights under this Agreement. Neither this Agreement nor the disclosure of any Confidential Information hereunder shall result in any obligation on the part of either party to enter into any further agreement with the other or to require Disclosing Party to disclose any particular or further Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change completely reverses the ownership and rights to the Confidential Information.  It grants the Recipient ownership, a full intellectual property license, and equal rights, directly contradicting the original intent of the NDA to protect the Disclosing Party's proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 654 states: 'The ownership of a thing is the right of one or more persons to possess and use it to the exclusion of others. In this code, the thing of which there may be ownership is called property.' By giving the Recipient ownership of the confidential information, the section now states that the Recipient has the right to possess and use the Disclosing Party's property, effectively negating the Disclosing Party's exclusive rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 654. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 2. PROPERTY [654 - 1422] ( Heading of Division 2 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 13. ) PART 1. PROPERTY IN GENERAL [654 - 749] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF PROPERTY [654 - 663] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 654. The ownership of a thing is the right of one or more persons to possess and use it to the exclusion of others. In this Code, the thing of which there may be ownership is called property. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts California Civil Code § 654, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text states the Disclosing Party retains exclusive property rights. The changed text grants the Recipient equal ownership, license, right, title, or interest in the Confidential Information, undermining the Disclosing Party's exclusive rights. This directly violates the law's definition of ownership as 'the right of one or more persons to possess and use it to the exclusion of others'. The modification does not just weaken the right; it provides the same right to another party, negating the 'exclusion of others' aspect, hence the 'YES' assessment.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement may cause irreparable damage to Disclosing Party and hereby agrees that, in addition to any other relief available at law or in equity, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that its breach of this Agreement will not cause irreparable damage to Disclosing Party and hereby agrees that, in addition to any other relief available at law or in equity, the Disclosing Party shall not be entitled to seek injunctive relief under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the acknowledgement of irreparable harm and removes the ability to seek injunctive relief. Injunctive relief is a common remedy for breach of confidentiality agreements because monetary damages are often inadequate to compensate for the loss of trade secrets or other confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 526 allows for injunctive relief '[w]hen it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a specified period or perpetually'. Removing the ability to seek injunctive relief could make the agreement unenforceable, especially since the nature of this NDA involves proprietary data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 526. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement and potentially relevant law. By stating that a breach will *not* cause irreparable harm and removing the ability to seek injunctive relief, the modified contract undermines the potential for the Disclosing Party to protect its confidential information, particularly since monetary damages may not be adequate. California Code of Civil Procedure § 526(a)(2), as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows for injunctive relief when 'the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action.' The changed text eliminates the acknowledgment of irreparable injury and the possibility of injunctive relief, creating a contradiction because the law permits injunctive relief in cases of irreparable injury, which is often applicable in NDA breaches.
